query: biomechanics of lag screw fixation in symphyseal fractures

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Ring Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Open reduction internal fixation (ORIF):
retrieved_docs.content: ORIF results in improved quality of reduction and better biomechanical stability compared to external fixation. Plates and screws across the symphysis can support the anterior ring. [22] Parasymphyseal pubic ramus fracture is treatable with open plating or percutaneous screw fixation. Sacroilial dislocation or sacral fracture can be treated with sacroiliac, transiliac transacral, or transiliac screws percutaneously. [23] A thorough understanding of pelvic ring osseous fixation pathways is necessary to perform these techniques safely. Sacral dysmorphism is common and can complicate safe percutaneous fixation of the posterior pelvic ring. [24] Open plating of sacroiliac joints or sacral fractures may be necessary if a closed reduction is unobtainable or the fracture pattern is not amenable to percutaneous fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Femur -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Nondisplaced fractures are treated with cancellous lag screws or a sliding hip screw. Both techniques have about the same outcomes and complications. Cancellous lag screws are associated with shorter operative time and less blood loss. However, cancellous lag screws have a higher revision rate than sliding hip screws due to prominent lag screw head irritation of soft tissue as the naturally femoral neck shortens postoperatively. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Indications -- Cannulated Screw Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Critical utilization of partially threaded (long or short thread options) screw options should be preoperatively planned before the procedure begins. The surgeon should plan on optimizing the "lag by design" technique. Recently, a femoral neck system implant has been described for the internal fixation of FNFs in young patients with theoretical biomechanical advantages compared to other implants. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Open reduction and internal fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior malleolus fractures can be addressed with a posteroanterior lag screw or an antiglide plate. Intraoperative assessment of the ankle syndesmosis is essential and can be performed using the Hook test (Cotton test) or intraoperative stress external rotation views to confirm proper alignment and stability. [20] [21]  The Hook test can be performed by the lateral distraction of the fibula with a bone hook under the image intensifier screen. The Hook test is considered positive if there is more than 2 mm of displacement of the fibula. [22]  If the hook test is positive and there is a syndesmosis diastasis, the syndesmosis should be reduced by applying a big reduction clamp to the medial and lateral malleoli and then fixed either by syndesmotic screws or tightropes.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Technique or Treatment -- Sliding Hip Screw
retrieved_docs.content: The lag screw is then applied over the wire and screwed in using the attached handle. The handle's final position should be parallel to the femur to ensure the lag screw will slot into the plating barrel. The plate is then slid over the lag screw. The plate template may be needed to ensure the plate is fully advanced. The guidewire can now be removed. The plate may be secured to the femur using a clamp.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Physeal Injuries Overview -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Physeal fracture displacement greater than 2 mm is an indication of open reduction, with pins and wires as the preferred fixation instruments. [18] Smooth pins are preferred over threaded ones, and small-diameter wires or pins are recommended. [19] Insertion at the physeal perimeter has a greater risk of growth arrest than insertion at the center. Titanium may cause physeal tethering more than stainless steel. Implants must be positioned parallel to the physis if the fracture geometry permits it. Additionally, positioning perpendicular to the physis will do less harm than placement at an oblique angle. Compression of transphyseal implants must be avoided. [20] Smith et al reported that 1 out of 5 patients who have had temporary transphyseal pinning for upper extremity fractures experienced physeal arrest. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Fracture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To appropriately apply the Young-Burgess classification system of pelvic ring injuries, clinicians must understand pelvic ligamentous anatomy. The bony pelvis is comprised of the ilium, ischium, and pubis. These structures form an anatomic ring with the sacrum. The symphyseal ligaments stabilize the pubic symphysis on the anterior side. The pelvic floor ligaments and posterior sacroiliac complex stabilize the pelvic ring on the posterior aspect. The sacrospinous and sacrotuberous ligaments of the pelvic floor, anterior to the sacroiliac joint, resist both shear and external rotation through the sacroiliac joint. The posterior sacroiliac complex is the most posterior ligament in the pelvic ring and the most essential structure for pelvic ring stability. Injury to the posterior ligaments reveals a very high-energy injury mechanism. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Pelvis Bones -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Timely operative fixation is key to managing unstable and severe pelvic fractures and should be undertaken as soon as possible following appropriate trauma resuscitation and stabilization. [23] The Young-Burgess classification is also useful for predicting transfusion requirements, which have been found to be increased in APC III, LC II, and LC III fractures compared to the other groups. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Femur Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Open Reduction Internal Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: The 95-degree blade plate is a useful implant for fracture fixation of OTA 33 type A patterns, as well as for the stabilization of malunions and nonunions.  The placement of the blade plate is often a technically demanding procedure because three planes must simultaneously be corrected. Condylar malalignment may result if the chisel and plate are incorrectly inserted. Sliding barrel condylar plates are devices based on the compression screw used for hip fractures. These devices allow for the ability to apply compression across the femoral condyles. They allow better purchase in osteoporotic bone and only need two planes of correction when compared to the blade plate. Potential disadvantages with this construct include its bulky size and the requirement to remove a large amount of bone from the lateral femoral condyle. Due to its bulky size, the plate may cause symptomatic hardware and knee symptoms. Clinical studies have generally shown good results with fixed angled blade plate and sliding barrel condylar plate devices with 82% reporting satisfactory to excellent results at 9.5 years for condylar plate and 81% good or excellent results for the dynamic condylar screw. [20] [21] A recent study comparing the less invasive stabilization system (LISS) with minimally invasive sliding barrel condylar plates in the treatment of OTA 33 type A1 to C2 concluded that there was no advantage for locking plate designs. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction -- Influential Factors and Variables for Construct Stiffness and Stability
retrieved_docs.content: One method of changing the stability is to alter the pin configuration. Placing pins closer to the fracture site, adding more pins and increasing the spread of the pins will all add to the stiffness of the construct. However, one must also place the pins out of the field of future surgical approaches during definitive fixation. Any increases in pin diameter will strengthen the construct to the fourth power and reduce the stress at the bone-pin interface. Increasing pin diameter has the greatest influence on the stability of unilateral frames. That said, larger pins increase the risk of a potential stress riser and can ultimately lead to fracture. [1] For example; a 5 mm pin is 144% stiffer than a 4 mm pin.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- Hemodynamically Unstable Pelvic Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Definitive repair, if needed, is undertaken once the patient has been adequately resuscitated and stabilized. Patients that are hemodynamically stable or “borderline” can be safely taken for definitive repair within 24 hours of injury. Physiologically deranged polytrauma patients should not be taken for definitive repair until they are stable. Studies show post-injury complication rates are increased if definitive repair is completed within four days of injury in polytrauma patients. Complications decreased when surgery was delayed until six to eight days post-injury. [42] Therefore, it is recommended to wait at least four days to proceed with any definitive treatment of pelvic fractures. Posterior ring injury reconstructions are most important for functional outcomes. APC I and LC I are managed nonoperatively. APC II is typically managed with anterior symphyseal plating. APC III is treated with either anterior symphyseal multihole plating or external fixation with posterior stabilization with sacroiliac screws. LC II is treated with ORIF of the ilium. LC III and VS require posterior stabilization with sacroiliac screws. It can be done open or percutaneous based. [43] [44] [45] Open reduction and internal fixation provides a better repair and allows earlier mobilization. Percutaneous fixation of the posterior ring can be useful in patients with traumatic posterior skin injuries. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians have been using external fixation to treat fractures for more than 2000 years after being first described by Hippocrates as a way to immobilize the fracture while preserving soft tissue integrity. The fixator design and biomechanics have changed dramatically over the years, but the principles remain the same. The primary goal of external fixation is to maintain the length, alignment, and rotation of the fracture. External fixation can serve as provisional fixation or definitive fixation purposes. Both methods can be performed in conjunction with partial internal fixation if necessary. It is important for orthopedic surgeons at a trauma center to be familiar with the techniques and principles of external fixation for various fractures of the upper extremity, lower extremity, and pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A dual-incision technique involving anteromedial and anterolateral approaches is commonly used to ensure accurate fracture reduction. An alternative sinus tarsi approach can be utilized as a lateral window, with a slightly more plantar incision from the distal fibula toward the 4th metatarsal. This approach provides an easier visualization of the lateral process. Exposures can be augmented with malleolar osteotomies to aid in visualizing the talar neck and body. Soft tissue considerations, fracture type, and other associated injuries often dictate the exact surgical approach. There are various reports of screw, plate, and hybrid fixation strategies. An estimated 96% of reported talar neck fractures were addressed with some combination of plate and screw constructs. [13] Longitudinal lag screws, especially on the medial side, are typically contraindicated because too much compression through medial comminution may result in a varus collapse or deformity. Published literature comparing screw constructs versus plate and screw constructs did not find one method superior to the other in terms of maintaining fixation and providing biomechanical stability. [11] Results from a study evaluated and compared screws alone versus screw and blade plate fixation, and the authors found no significant differences in yield point, stiffness, or load to create a 3 mm deformation. [12] This study utilized conventional 3.5 mm cortical or 4.0 mm cancellous screws. However, the authors suggest that headless variable-pitch screws would improve fixation strength compared to the conventional cannulated screws used in their investigation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Distal Tibiofibular Joint (Tibiofibular Syndesmosis) -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of the surgical technique utilized, the goal of syndesmotic repair/fixation is to achieve an anatomic reduction of the syndesmosis.  Studies have previously demonstrated anatomic reduction is crucial to successful outcomes in patients. [14] A 2018 meta-analysis comparing suture button (i.e., tightrope) fixation versus screw fixation for syndesmosis injuries reported the former technique resulted in improved functional outcomes as well as lower rates of broken implant and joint malreduction compared to the screw fixation patients. [15] Nonetheless, similar rates of postoperative complications were seen otherwise when comparing the two treatment groups.  Other studies have also reported syndesmotic malreduction utilizing screw fixation syndesmotic repair techniques can occur in up to 52% of cases, leading to poor functional outcomes. [1] [11] Typically, two 3.5 mm or 4.5 mm syndesmotic screws placed 2 to 5 cm above the joint line is the preferred method of fixation, but there are no reports of a significant improvement in outcomes for using one versus two screws or specific screw sizes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Thigh Sartorius Muscle -- Clinical Significance -- Anterior Superior Iliac Spine (ASIS) Avulsion
retrieved_docs.content: Open reduction internal fixation (ORIF) is indicated when the avulsion of the ASIS displaces by a distance of more than 2 or 3 centimeters. Painful non-unions are also an indication for surgical fixation. There are several different options, including lag screw and tension band fixation. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation has been used for the treatment of fractures for 2000 years. The application and devises have changed, but the biomechanics remain the same. This activity outlines the relevant anatomy, techniques, and fixation goals and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients who undergo external fixation.

Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

Within this framework, epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—target the growth plates of the distal femur, proximal tibia, and fibula to halt or partially halt growth and correct angular deformities such as genu varus or valgus. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 950
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific surgical treatment used for managing fractures or dislocations of several long bones, including the hip and femur as well as the radius and ulna. It is classified as a particular form of internal fixation of a fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), indicating that it belongs to the broader category of internal fixation procedures performed without prior fracture reduction. Additionally, this procedure is considered a subtype of internal fixation techniques applied to the clavicle without fracture reduction, reflecting its inclusion within the general family of bone‑specific internal fixation methods. Together, these relationships highlight that internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a targeted operative approach that fits within larger treatment categories for various skeletal injuries, emphasizing its role in orthopedic care for multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: relationship” connection are epiphyseal arrest (any method, e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia or fibula, which are used to halt or partially halt growth to correct angular deformities such as genu varus or genu valgus. Collectively, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of femoral surgical care—from acute fracture fixation and malunion repair to limb‑lengthening, shortening, growth‑plate modulation, prosthetic revision, and prophylactic measures—providing clinicians with a hierarchical framework for selecting the appropriate procedure based on anatomical site, desired mechanical outcome, and overall treatment context.

Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1188
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity other than the hip or femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. This procedure is also identified by the Arabic term تثبيت داخلي للكسر, indicating that the two expressions refer to the same orthopedic intervention. Within this hierarchical framework, internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction is a specific subtype that falls under the broader category of internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction. Together, these relationships illustrate how internal fixation without reduction serves as a parent treatment modality for multiple anatomic sites and includes more specialized procedures such as clavicle fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions for the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated lower‑extremity structures. Child procedures include growth‑modulating surgeries such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which alter physeal activity to correct angular deformities. Fracture‑stabilization techniques directly targeting the femur are represented by “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation.” Osteoplastic procedures modify femoral length or contour and include “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment for both fracture or dislocation of the hip and femur and for fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity other than the hip or femur. This procedure is closely related to the Arabic‑language concept تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of fracture), which is considered the inverse of the English term, indicating that they represent reciprocal perspectives on the same surgical approach. Moreover, Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific form of Internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, highlighting its applicability to clavicular injuries when reduction is not performed. Both تثبيت داخلي للكسر and إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip head replacement) are listed as members of the SMQ category هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (osteoporosis/low bone mass), underscoring the clinical relevance of these surgical interventions in patients with compromised bone quality. Together, these relationships illustrate how internal fixation procedures, including those without fracture reduction, fit within broader treatment categories for lower‑extremity injuries and are particularly pertinent to the management of osteoporotic bone conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.
